steps.title: Identify what follows the blank
steps.content: After the blank, “such as the scent of roses when roses appeared in a scene” is a nonrestrictive (non-essential) example — it illustrates one of the odors the vents emitted but is not required for the sentence to be grammatical or complete. Such a phrase must be set off from the preceding independent clause with appropriate punctuation.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice and confirm the answer
steps.content:
• Choice A — movie such: no punctuation before “such as” — the nonessential example is not set off from the clause, producing a run-on sentence. ✗
  • Choice B — movie; such: a semicolon can only join two independent clauses; “such as…scene” is not an independent clause, so a semicolon here is a punctuation error. ✗
  • Choice C — movie. Such: a period turns “Such as…scene” into a standalone sentence, but it is not an independent clause — this creates a sentence fragment. ✗
  • Choice D — movie, such: a comma correctly sets off the nonrestrictive example from the independent clause, signaling it as additional clarification rather than an essential definition. ✓
That is the correct answer.
